text: # Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: Inland shipping is facing a change of course. Belgium is gaining ground as a destination for liquid bulk, especially chemicals and biofuels, while inland shipping to Germany transports less due to the reduction in coal and steel. In addition to this shift, there are challenges such as fluctuating water levels and the threat of overcapacity in the fleet.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: 173 KB](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/ Tussen-de-getijden-houdt-de-binnenvaart-koers.pdf)

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: Inland shipping is important for international transport. The most important destinations are Germany and Belgium, which together account for 96 percent of the volume. Total inland waterway transport, the freight transport between Dutch loading points and foreign unloading points, has decreased considerably in recent years. This decrease almost exclusively concerns transport to Germany, which has fallen sharply. On the other hand, exports to Belgium have actually increased in recent years. As a result, the transport volumes of both countries are increasingly moving to a comparable level. Belgium appears to be playing an increasingly prominent role in goods trade with the Netherlands via inland shipping.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: Total inland waterway transport, the freight transport between Dutch loading points and foreign unloading points, has decreased considerably in recent years. This decrease almost exclusively concerns transport to Germany and Belgium, which together account for [more than 96%](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/nl/dataset/85527NED/table?ts=1763110101914) of the total volume. Exports to Germany in particular have fallen sharply. On the other hand, exports to Belgium have actually increased in recent years. As a result, the transport volumes of both countries are increasingly moving to a comparable level. Belgium appears to be playing an increasingly prominent role in goods trade with the Netherlands via inland shipping.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The decrease to Germany is mainly attributed to the decrease in the discharge of coal and metal ores, as a result of the phasing out of coal-fired power stations. However, during the corona period, a temporary increase in the use of coal took place in early 2022. This was an emergency measure to generate electricity following the Russian invasion of Ukraine and the subsequent energy crisis. These conditions caused a temporary increase in discharge. At the same time, high energy prices put energy-intensive industries in Germany, such as the steel industry, under significant pressure. This reduced demand for metal ores, leading to a drop in output over the course of 2022.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The increase to Belgium mainly consists of liquid bulk, especially refined petroleum products such as naphtha and fuel oil. These products belong to the petrochemical industry and form the basis for the production of chemical raw materials, such as plastics. According to [AMS Barging](https://www.amsbarging.com/), specialized in transport and bunkering of liquid bulk, the share of biofuels is also increasing, such as FAME: a fuel produced from animal or vegetable oils and fats.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The chemical industry of the port of Antwerp is under pressure. High energy prices weaken Europe's competitive position, while countries such as the United States and China produce at rock bottom prices. Belgium is therefore importing more and more chemicals, which creates a greater supply on the market and puts pressure on producers. For inland shipping, this means a higher demand for tankers for supply and delivery between terminals and the hinterland. Ships can also serve as storage facilities on the water, which is known in the sector as 'floating storage'.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: In addition to production, storage is an important part of a port as a hub. The wet bulk storage capacity of the port of Antwerp has grown significantly over the past ten years (14 percent) and peaked during the corona pandemic, while that of Rotterdam has only increased slightly (4 percent). Rotterdam focuses mainly on fossil fuels such as oil and gas, while Antwerp benefits from various niches such as chemicals and biofuels. The new storage capacity and diversification in chemical products create smaller, more frequent flows, which are ideal for transport by inland shipping. Fossil fuels, such as oil, are often transported via pipelines to other parts of Europe, including Belgium.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: However, the tank storage capacity of the port of Antwerp has decreased significantly over the past two years. A possible cause of this is the closure of various chemical companies in the region. These companies played a role in both production and storage of chemical raw materials and products, so their closure not only leads to a decline in production, but also to reduced storage capacity.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: Another explanation for the growth of inland shipping transport to Belgium is the increasing demand for biofuels. These are fuels made from organic material, such as vegetable oils, and are an alternative to fossil fuels. Examples are bioethanol, FAME and HVO, a renewable diesel made from animal or vegetable oils and fats. Consumption of biofuels in Europe has increased in recent years. To further encourage this use, the blending obligation has been increased from 10.5 percent in 2024 to 12.2 percent in 2025. This percentage is expected to [rise (pdf)](https://www.petrolfed.be/sites/default/files/editor/ENERGIA_Persbericht%20kernfigures%202024_0.pdf) to 29 percent in 2030, which may further increase the demand for biofuels.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The total discharge of dry bulk is still at a low level, but has not decreased further in the past year. The German industry will invest heavily in infrastructure, which may give a boost to Dutch dry bulk inland shipping in the future. This concerns goods such as sand, gravel and cement. Liquid bulk discharge has fallen over the past quarter. The inland shipping sector is partly under pressure due to low water levels due to climate change, which results in fluctuating inland shipping rates and requires a lot of flexibility in fleet capacity. In contrast to dry bulk ships, wet bulk ships are more affected by low water levels, because these ships are heavier in themselves, i.e. without cargo.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The total Dutch inland shipping fleet, in terms of numbers of ships under the Dutch flag, has decreased in recent years, mainly due to a decrease in the number of dry bulk ships. The number of wet bulk vessels, on the other hand, has remained relatively stable. At the same time, [the share of larger ships](https://www.cbs.nl/nl-nl/visualisaties/verkeer-en-verkeer/voermiddels-en-infrastructure/binnenvaartschips) has actually increased, which, in combination with fluctuating water levels, can entail additional risks. For example, at high tide bridges or other obstacles may pose a problem, while at low tide the channels may not be deep enough. According to the international inland shipping association IVR, the Dutch fleet currently consists of approximately 1,000 tankers and 3,800 dry bulk ships.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: ### Development of the European inland shipping fleet

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: Analysis of the total European fleet development, or the difference between the inflow of new ships and the outflow due to scrapping within a fleet, shows that the number of wet bulk ships has undergone a positive development over the years. This indicates that more of these types of ships are being built than being demolished. However, the market for second-hand inland vessels did reach [a peak] in 2022(https://www.nt.nl/binnenvaart/2022/08/15/makelaars-in-tweedehands-binnenvaartschips-beleven-gouden-tijden/), partly driven by demand from Eastern Europe as a result of the grain trade in Ukraine. Although no exact dates are available, it is estimated that several dozen ships were exported during the most active period. In addition to demolition, the sale of second-hand ships can contribute to the decrease in the total number of inland vessels.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The increase in the number of larger vessels, combined with a more or less stable fleet of wet bulk vessels in recent years, has led to a growth in total capacity within the market. When shipping companies respond to high freight rates by ordering additional ships, this expansion only becomes available years later. By then, a lot of new capacity could come onto the market at the same time, possibly creating an oversupply. There is a risk that this delayed adjustment process, known as the pig cycle, will cause freight rates to fall.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: The outlook for liquid bulk shipping is twofold, with both opportunities and challenges emerging across segments. Our expectation is that growth will be moderate, especially in chemical goods, where inland shipping can benefit from higher global imports as European production declines due to high gas prices. At the same time, the energy transition offers both opportunities and risks; While the volume of petroleum products such as petrol and diesel will shrink, biofuels offer new perspectives thanks to their suitability for use in inland shipping.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: In addition, the flexibility in the deployment of ships remains largely dependent on varying water levels. Scaling up in the sector has led to an increase in total loading capacity, but also entails a shift in the availability of smaller ships. This can lead to scarcity on secondary waterways, while capacity on main axes such as the ARA-Rhine region remains largely stable.

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: Thanks to [AMS Barging](https://www.amsbarging.com/).

metadata.source_title: Inland shipping keeps its course between the tides

text: After a difficult year, in which international trade declined due to a rapid reduction of excess inventories, the transport sector has started a cautious recovery. At the beginning of 2024, freight volumes increased in both container shipping and aviation. Thanks to the improving economic growth, the sector can recover further. However, the recovery is being held back by lower demand for building materials. In the meantime, road transport will have to deal with a pile-up of environmental regulations in the coming years.
